text:
Thank you, Brother Boze. I could just've listened to that a while. That's very fine.
I'm sorry that I was late tonight. The manager called me up and told me to come a little early tonight. And I forgot about it. And I was just going ahead praying and I happened to look up and I think, "Why, here it's…" I looked at my watch and I said, "Well, my! it's quarter after, I got to hurry."
And here they was standing out there to meet me when I come in. So they said they was going to dock me. So I guess that's fine. All right.
text_no:
Takk, Bror Boze. Jeg kunne ha lyttet til det en stund til. Det var veldig fint.
Jeg beklager at jeg var sen i kveld. Lederen ringte meg og ba meg komme litt tidligere i kveld, men jeg glemte det. Jeg fortsatte bare å be og kikket tilfeldigvis opp, og tenkte: "Jøss, her er det…" Jeg så på klokken og sa: "Min, oh min! Klokken er kvart over, jeg må skynde meg."
Og der stod de og ventet på meg da jeg kom inn. De sa de ville trekke meg i lønn. Så jeg antar det er greit. Helt i orden.

text:
Well, we're happy to be here tonight. Very happy and sorry that the weather keeps on like this. But we understand that tomorrow or the next day it's going to break, according to the weather man. I hope that he's right. But whatever, the Holy Spirit will meet with us just the same. He never fails us and He'll always be here just the same.
I love Him tonight. And I know you do too, don't you? I'm very happy that everybody's in love with Jesus. And He's in love with every one of us. That's the good part (Isn't it?) that He loved us before we loved Him.
When we were real ugly behaving and contrary to God, yet He loved us. That's sounds bad, doesn't it? To think that … Brother Ryan, I don't know whether I could suffer that very long or not: Somebody being so ugly and different with me and still I love them.
text_no:
Vi er glade for å være her i kveld. Veldig glade, selv om vi beklager at været er som det er. Vi har hørt at det skal bli bedre i morgen eller dagen etter, i følge værmeldingen. Jeg håper de har rett. Uansett, Den Hellige Ånd vil møte oss uansett. Han svikter oss aldri og vil alltid være her.
Jeg elsker Ham i kveld. Og jeg vet at dere gjør det samme, ikke sant? Jeg er veldig glad for at alle elsker Jesus. Og Han elsker hver og en av oss. Det er den gode delen, ikke sant? At Han elsket oss før vi elsket Ham.
Da vi oppførte oss virkelig stygt og var motstridende til Gud, elsket Han oss likevel. Det høres ille ut, ikke sant? Å tenke på det... Bror Ryan, jeg vet ikke om jeg kunne tålt det lenge: At noen var så uvennlige og forskjellige mot meg, og at jeg likevel elsket dem.

text:
Here one time (hasn't been too long ago) I was having a meeting up in Ohio. And oh, the big arena was packed and we was just… I had to stay way out in the country at a tourist court. And we'd been eating at a little Dunkard restaurant.
Oh, it was the nicest people and the people were so kind, and we appreciated them so much. And then Sunday of course they closed up. We had to go across the street … or, across the road, to a little place. It was a very worldly little place, had one of them little noisy juke boxes that everybody played.
Brother, the guy that will take them out of the nation, I'll vote for him. I sure will. And all that there crazy music going on. And so, as soon as I walked in over there, the first thing I noticed was a policeman standing with his arm around a young lady, playing a slot machine.
text_no:
En gang, for ikke så lenge siden, hadde jeg et møte i Ohio. Den store arenaen var fullpakket, og jeg måtte bo langt ute på landet på et motell. Vi hadde spist på en liten Dunkard-restaurant der folk var så vennlige. Vi satte stor pris på dem.
På søndagen stengte restauranten, så vi måtte gå over veien til et lite sted. Det var et veldig verdslig sted med en liten, bråkete jukebox som alle spilte på. 
Den personen som får fjernet dem fra landet, får min stemme, helt sikkert. Alt det tullete musikken laget mye støy. Så snart jeg gikk inn der, la jeg merke til en politimann som sto med armen rundt en ung dame mens han spilte på en spilleautomat.

text:
And that's illegal in Ohio, to play a slot machine. But the law… Then the next thing I noticed was, looked back there and a young lady very indecently dressed, and sitting on the side of the table about half drunk, and some boys with her.
And looked sitting down to my right, there was an elderly lady about, oh, sixty-five, I guess. And she had a little … very half-nude clothes on, you know. And her lips was painted with blue-looking paint, and her toe nails the same color. And, oh, my! I thought, That's terrible.
And I begin to think, Is my little Sarah and Rebekah going to have to be raised up such as that? What will be the outcome? I thought, O God how can you stand to look at it? Looks like you'd just turn on and blow the world up.
text_no:
Det er ulovlig i Ohio å spille på spilleautomater. Deretter la jeg merke til en ung dame, veldig uanstendig kledd og halvveis beruset, sittende ved siden av bordet med noen gutter.
Til høyre for meg satt det en eldre dame, omtrent sekstifem år, vil jeg tro. Hun hadde på seg veldig avslørende klær, og hennes lepper var malt med blåfarget sminke, samme farge på tåneglene. Å, herregud! Jeg tenkte, dette er forferdelig.
Jeg begynte å tenke: Må mine små Sarah og Rebekah vokse opp i et samfunn som dette? Hva vil bli utfallet? Jeg tenkte, å Gud, hvordan kan Du holde ut å se på dette? Det ser ut som Du bare ville slå på bryteren og sprenge verden.

text:
And then while I was standing there looking at that, I was criticizing that woman, I was criticizing the officer---in my heart. But I had never taken a seat yet at the table. And then just seemed like Something said to me, "Step over here to one side. I want to talk to you." That was my Boss.
And when I stood over there, and I begin to think like this. And it just seemed to come to me that … before me, not a vision; just a mental vision, I'd call it.
Seemed like I could see the world; and around the world was a rainbow. And that rainbow represented the blood of Jesus Christ. And if that blood would ever leave there, God couldn't look at the world a second. He'd destroy it. That blood of Christ is the only thing that keeps the wrath of God off us tonight. Did you know that?
If it wasn't for that, there'd be no more world in five minutes from now. God cannot look in the face of sin. And the reason He can't see the world is because the blood of Jesus Christ is holding it off---the sin.
text_no:
Mens jeg sto der og betraktet situasjonen, kritiserte jeg kvinnen og politibetjenten i mitt stille sinn. Men jeg hadde ennå ikke satt meg ved bordet. Så var det som om noe sa til meg: "Kom hit til siden. Jeg vil snakke med deg." Det var min Herre.
Da jeg sto der, begynte jeg å tenke. Og det virket som om en mental visjon kom til meg. Jeg så for meg verden, og rundt verden var det en regnbue. Den regnbuen representerte Jesu Kristi blod. Hvis det blodet noensinne forsvant, kunne Gud ikke se på verden i et sekund uten å ødelegge den. Det er Kristi blod som holder Guds vrede borte fra oss i kveld. Visste du det?
Uten det blodet ville det ikke være noen verden om fem minutter. Gud kan ikke se på syndens ansikt. Grunnen til at Han ikke kan se verden er fordi Jesu Kristi blod holder synden borte.

text:
If God can look in and see such a scene as that, it'd be finished. He's too holy, He couldn't stand it. Then… But the blood of Jesus…
Then here's what come to me. I thought to see the blood of Jesus, how it acted as a bumper. Just like to the car. The car instead of, when it strikes, it will hit the bumper, and the bumper will protect the car. I thought, That's what the blood of Jesus is. It's a bumper for our sins; that before it goes before God, He catches it. It's a bumper."
And I thought, Oh my! Here I was criticizing that woman. I may not have did that. I may not have acted like that policeman was acting. But yet, in the sight of God, I was a sinner just as bad as they was. Just as bad as the woman sitting there. Sin is sin. That's all, see.
text_no:
Hvis Gud kunne se en slik scene, ville det vært over. Han er for hellig til å tåle det. Men Jesu blod...
Så kom dette til meg: Jeg tenkte på Jesu blod og hvordan det fungerer som en støtdemper. Akkurat som på en bil; når bilen treffer noe, tar støtdemperen slaget og beskytter bilen. Jeg tenkte, det er hva Jesu blod gjør. Det er en støtdemper for våre synder; før synden når Gud, fanger Han den. Det er en støtdemper.
Og jeg tenkte: Oi, her satt jeg og kritiserte den kvinnen. Jeg har kanskje ikke gjort det samme, kanskje ikke oppført meg som den politimannen, men i Guds øyne var jeg en synder like ille som dem. Like ille som kvinnen som satt der. Synd er synd. Det er alt.

text:
No matter what your immorals are, you're a sinner. You're just a sinner.
And so, I thought, Yes, and God would have destroyed me if it hadn't been for the blood of Jesus Christ, keeping my sins from His presence.
And then Something come to me and said, "Yes, I forgive you, but you want to condemn her."
I thought, God, that's right. That's right.
I seen my sins then, and one day I crawled up to Him, and I seen an old book laying there, it had William Branham at the top and all kinds of sins wrote under it. And I said, "Lord, I'm so sorry that I've caused You all this bleeding." And His face sweating and blood running down.
text_no:
Uansett hva dine umoralske handlinger er, så er du en synder. Du er bare en synder. Så tenkte jeg, ja, og Gud ville ha ødelagt meg dersom det ikke hadde vært for Jesu Kristi blod, som holder mine synder borte fra Hans nærvær. 
Da kom det Noe til meg og sa: "Ja, Jeg tilgir deg, men du vil fordømme henne." Jeg tenkte, Gud, det stemmer. Det stemmer. Jeg så mine synder da, og en dag krøp jeg opp til Ham. Jeg så en gammel bok ligge der, med William Branham øverst og alle slags synder skrevet under. Jeg sa: "Herre, jeg er så lei meg for at jeg har påført Deg all denne blødningen." Og Hans ansikt svettet, og blodet rant ned.

text:
And I said, "I was the one who did that. Would you forgive me?" And He took His hand and dipped it in His side and wrote on that old book "Pardoned," throwed it back behind Him in the Sea of Forgetfulness. Never to remember it against me no more. That's right.
Now He forgave me and I wanted to condemn her. That changed my attitude. I walked down there to the woman. I said, "How do you do?" There was two men with her and they were very, very bad acting. And so they just stepped out. And I walked down and I said, "Would you pardon me?"
And she's drinking. And she said, "Yes, sir."
I said, "I'd like to talk to you." I said, "I'm a minister."
She said, "Yes?"
And I said, "I am." I said, "Wonder if I could ask you a question. Why does this be like this? Are you a mother?"
She said, "Yes sir."
I said, "Why would you be drinking and things? May I ask, if it's not getting too personal with you?"
Well, if you'd have heard the woman's story, it was a heart-broken story. And she said, "I was once a Christian." Said, "If I'm not mistaken, aren't you Rev. Branham that was down there?"
I said, "I am."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Det var jeg som gjorde det. Vil du tilgi meg?" Og Han tok Sin hånd, dyppet den i Sin side og skrev på den gamle boken "Benådet," før Han kastet den bak Seg i Glemselens hav. Aldri mer å huske det mot meg. Det stemmer.
Nå hadde Han tilgitt meg, og jeg ville fordømme henne. Det endret min holdning. Jeg gikk ned til kvinnen og sa, "Hvordan har du det?" Det var to menn med henne, og de oppførte seg veldig dårlig. De trakk seg unna, og jeg gikk bort og sa, "Vil du tilgi meg?"
Hun var i ferd med å drikke. Hun sa, "Ja, sir."
Jeg sa, "Jeg vil gjerne snakke med deg." Jeg sa, "Jeg er en forkynner."
Hun sa, "Ja?"
Jeg sa, "Det er jeg." Jeg sa, "Lurer på om jeg kan stille deg et spørsmål. Hvorfor er det slik? Er du en mor?"
Hun sa, "Ja, sir."
Jeg fortsatte, "Hvorfor drikker du og gjør slike ting? Kan jeg spørre, hvis det ikke blir for personlig?"
Hvis du hadde hørt kvinnens historie, ville du forstått at den var hjerteskjærende. Hun sa, "Jeg var en gang en kristen." Og hun la til, "Hvis jeg ikke tar feil, er ikke du Pastor Branham som var der nede?"
Jeg sa, "Det er jeg."

text:
And I told her what I'd just seen. I said, "I stood here and condemned you, and thought God ought to come in and tear the place up, with such as this going on, but I've changed my mind. God forgave me. And I won't even forgive you." And right there at that seat, I led her to Jesus Christ. Amen.
See, it's your attitude towards things. Don't condemn others. If they do wrong, be good to them anyhow. Just look over those things, because God has had to look over a whole lot for you and I. That's right.
God help us to have that attitude always in our hearts. You pray for me that I'll never look upon people like that no more, no matter what they're doing; that I'll always look up on them that they're creatures of God; that God sent His Son to take their place.
And me as a minister, I'm grateful that He forgive me. And I want Him to forgive them also. That's the way we should do it.
text_no:
Jeg fortalte henne hva jeg nettopp hadde sett. Jeg sa: "Jeg sto her og fordømte deg, og tenkte at Gud burde komme inn og ødelegge stedet med slikt som dette på gang, men jeg har ombestemt meg. Gud tilga meg. Og jeg vil ikke engang tilgi deg." Akkurat der på setet ledet jeg henne til Jesus Kristus. Amen.
Det handler om din holdning til ting. Ikke fordøm andre. Hvis de gjør noe galt, vær god mot dem uansett. Se bort fra slike ting, for Gud har måttet se bort fra mye for deg og meg. Det er riktig.
Må Gud hjelpe oss å ha den holdningen alltid i våre hjerter. Be for meg at jeg aldri vil se på mennesker på den måten igjen, uansett hva de gjør; at jeg alltid vil se på dem som Guds skapninger; at Gud sendte Sin Sønn for å ta deres plass.
Som en forkynner er jeg takknemlig for at Han tilga meg. Og jeg vil at Han skal tilgi dem også. Det er slik vi bør gjøre.

text:
In Isaiah the 53rd chapter we read this:
Who hath believed our report? and to whom is the arm of the LORD revealed?
For he shall grow up before him as a tender plant, and as a root out of … dry ground: he hath no form nor comeliness; and when we shall see him, there is no beauty that we should desire him.
He is despised and rejected of men; a man of sorrow, … acquainted with grief: and we hid as it was our faces from him; he was despised, and we esteemed him not.
Surely he has borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows: yet we did esteem him stricken, smitten of God, and afflicted.
But he was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquity: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed.
All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way; and the LORD hath laid up on him the iniquity of us all.
He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth: he was brought as lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he opened not his mouth.
He was taken from prison … from judgment: and who shall declare his generation? for he was cut off out of the land of the living: and for the transgressions of my people was he stricken.
And he made his grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death; because he had done no violence, neither was there deceit found in his mouth.
Yet it pleased the LORD to bruise him; he hath put him to grief: when thou shalt make his soul an offering for sin, he shall see his seed, and he shall prolong his days, and the pleasure of the LORD shall prosper in his hand.
May the Lord add His blessings to the reading of His Word.
The prophet Isaiah getting a vision of what Jesus was, who He was, and what He would do; and what a sin offering He was made.
text_no:
I Jesaja kapittel 53 leser vi følgende:
Hvem trodde det budskap vi hørte? Og for hvem ble Herrens arm åpenbart?
Han skjøt opp som en kvist for Hans åsyn, som et rotskudd av tørr jord. Han hadde ingen skjønnhet og prakt; når vi så Ham, kunne vi ikke begjære Ham.
Foraktet var Han og forlatt av mennesker, en smertenes mann, vel kjent med sykdom. Vi skjulte liksom vårt ansikt for Ham, foraktet, og vi aktet Ham for intet.
Men sannelig, våre sykdommer har Han tatt på Seg, og våre smerter bar Han. Vi trodde at Han var blitt rammet, slått av Gud og plaget.
Men Han ble såret for våre overtredelser, knust for våre misgjerninger. Straffen lå på Ham for at vi skulle ha fred, og ved Hans sår har vi fått legedom.
Vi fór alle vill som sauer, vi vendte oss hver til sin vei. Men Herren lot skylden som lå på oss alle, ramme Ham.
Han ble mishandlet, men Han bar det ydmykt. Han åpnet ikke sin munn, likesom et lam som føres bort for å slaktes, og likesom en sau som tier når den klippes.
Fra fengsel og dom ble Han tatt bort, og hvem tenkte vel at Han ble utryddet av de levendes land? For mitt folks overtredelse ble plagen lagt på Ham.
De ga Ham en grav blant ugudelige, og blant de rike i Hans død, enda Han ikke hadde gjort noen urett, og det ikke var svik i Hans munn.
Det behaget Herren å knuse Ham; Han slo Ham med sykdom. Når Du gjør Hans sjel til et skyldoffer, skal Han se sine etterkommere og leve lenge, og Herrens vilje skal ha framgang ved Hans hånd.
Må Herren legge Sin velsignelse til lesningen av Hans Ord.
Profeten Jesaja fikk en visjon av hva Jesus skulle være, hvem Han var, og hva Han ville gjøre; og for et syndoffer Han skulle bli.

text:
It's awful warm tonight. So I believe instead of preaching as a I said I would… My preaching: I'm not very much of a preacher. I'm just one of these old kind of sassafras (we call it down in Indiana, you know.) So you're too nice of a people to have to push that off on. So I'll just testify, and tell you something maybe that the Lord has did along our journey.
Now, I want to think of some of my early days. When I first started out in the great campaigns, the meetings had a lot of tinsel then. Of course, everybody was running here and there. But now, of course it's different than what it was then.
The pastor, Baxter, my brother and I were talking the other day, thinking, said, "Look at the great things, what's happened, then look how it settles down." God gives a meeting and then He finishes up His things.
We think of Jesus Christ, when He was here on earth. His first years of His ministry, oh, the tinsel was hanging everywhere. The second year of His ministry, they'd begin to accuse Him and call Him "Beelzebub" and so forth. The third year, they killed Him.
text_no:
Det er veldig varmt i kveld. Så istedenfor å forkynne som jeg sa at jeg ville... Å forkynne: Jeg er ikke mye av en forkynner. Jeg er bare av den gamle typen (vi kaller det sassafras nede i Indiana, vet du). Dere er altfor gode mennesker til å måtte høre det fra meg. Så jeg skal bare vitne og fortelle dere noe Herren har gjort langs vår reise.
Nå vil jeg tenke tilbake på noen av mine tidlige dager. Da jeg først begynte med de store kampanjene, hadde møtene mye glitter. Selvsagt, alle løp hit og dit. Men nå er det annerledes enn det var dengang.
Pastoren, Baxter, min Bror og jeg snakket sammen her om dagen. Vi tenkte: "Se på de store tingene som har skjedd, og se hvordan det roer seg ned." Gud gir et møte og avslutter deretter Sine ting.
Vi tenker på Jesus Kristus da Han var her på jorden. I de første årene av Hans tjeneste, åh, var det glitter overalt. I det andre året av Hans tjeneste begynte de å anklage Ham og kalle Ham "Beelzebub" og så videre. Det tredje året drepte de Ham.

text:
See, that's the way it goes always. The first years of the Pentecostal church, it was wonderful. Right away there was a persecution come in the second year and scattered them everywhere. The third and fourth year they begin to find people---just a little bitty groups, like there was five men besides four or five women. That was a church.
See how it is? It's always that way. Just God sends out a great gospel net and He throws it… The kingdom of heaven is like unto a man who went to the sea and threw in a net. And when he drew the net in, he had everything that wallowed in the sea---he had it in the net. He had fish, he had turtles, he had water spiders, he had serpents, he had everything.
Now, it's not our business as preachers to say, "This is fish, and this is that." We just pull the gospel net, and then it isn't long (when you see a revival go on) many people get saved and praise the Lord. But see, some of those are fish. And some of them are … we'd say "turtles." It isn't very long till you'd say, "Well, I didn't believe there was anything to it after all."
And here he goes running right on back into the puddle again.
text_no:
Slik er det alltid. De første årene av pinsebevegelsen var fantastiske. Straks kom det en forfølgelse i det andre året, som spredte dem overalt. I det tredje og fjerde året begynte de å finne folk—bare små grupper, som fem menn og fire-fem kvinner. Det var en menighet.
Ser du hvordan det er? Det er alltid slik. Gud sender ut et stort evangelienett og kaster det ut... Himlenes rike er som en mann som kastet et nett i havet. Da han dro nettet inn, hadde han alt som var i havet—fisker, skilpadder, vannedderkopper, slanger, alt sammen.
Nå, det er ikke vår oppgave som forkynnere å si: "Dette er fisk, og dette er noe annet." Vi trekker bare evangelienettet, og så tar det ikke lang tid (når du ser en vekkelse på gang) før mange blir frelst og priser Herren. Men ser du, noen av dem er fisker. Og noen av dem er... vi ville sagt "skilpadder." Det tar ikke lang tid før du sier: "Vel, jeg trodde ikke det var noe i det likevel."
Og så går de tilbake til dammen igjen.

text:
The old water spider, you know. Plop, plop, plop. "Told you that wasn't right after all." Away he goes back to the water.
And the serpent will start hissing, saying, "I don't like the way they do these things." Sss-Sss. Right on back to the water it goes. But there's some fish in there too. God knows. They're fish by nature, he's a spider by nature, he's a serpent by nature.
text_no:
Den gamle vannløperen, du vet. Plopp, plopp, plopp. "Sa jeg ikke at det ikke var riktig til slutt." Svupper tilbake til vannet.
Og slangen vil begynne å hvese, siende, "Jeg liker ikke måten de gjør disse tingene på." Sss-Sss. Rett tilbake til vannet går den. Men der er også noen fisker. Gud vet. De er fisker av natur, han er en løper av natur, han er en slange av natur.

text:
And God is the one who does the election and the calling. And it's just our business to preach the Gospel. Is that right? God does the calling. One plants, another one waters, and God takes the increase.
Now remember, it was a very hard thing at first when I first started out preaching divine healing. Oh my! I remember what the bishop of the church told me. Why, he said, "Billy you had a nightmare. You never seen no Angel."
I said, "I don't appreciate that, Brother Davis." I said, "No, sir. To me it's sacred and it means a-lots to me."
He said, "You mean you with your grammar school education is going to pray for kings and monarchs?"
I said, "That's what He said. It will be that way."
Said, "Go on, Billy." And so…
I said, "No, I'll just give you my fellowship card while I'm standing here. Because I'm not going to be using it anymore, I'm afraid."
So, he said, "Oh, I wouldn't think that way."
But, I said, "Well, we just might as well." I said, "If God is sending me, there's going to be somebody out there's going to receive it." That's all. God will never work anything here but what He's got something working there. Do you believe that? Absolutely.
text_no:
Og Gud er den som står for utvelgelsen og kallelsen. Vår oppgave er å forkynne Evangeliet. Er det riktig? Gud kaller. Én planter, en annen vanner, og Gud gir vekst.
Husk at det var veldig vanskelig i begynnelsen da jeg først startet med å forkynne guddommelig helbredelse. Å, herlighet! Jeg husker hva biskopen i menigheten sa til meg. Han sa: "Billy, du hadde et mareritt. Du så ingen Engel."
Jeg svarte: "Jeg setter ikke pris på det, Bror Davis. For meg er det hellig, og det betyr mye for meg."
Han sa: "Mener du at du, med din grunnskoleutdannelse, skal be for konger og monarker?"
Jeg sa: "Det var det Han sa. Slik vil det bli."
Han svarte: "Gå videre, Billy." Og så…
Jeg sa: "Nei, jeg gir deg bare mitt medlemskort nå, fordi jeg er redd jeg ikke vil bruke det mer."
Han sa: "Å, jeg ville ikke tenke slik."
Men jeg sa: "Vel, vi kan like godt gjøre det. Hvis Gud sender meg, vil det være noen der ute som vil motta det." Det er alt. Gud gjør aldri noe her uten å ha noe arbeid der. Tror du på det? Absolutt.

text:
Today I was thinking. And a man in his heart … as he thinks in his heart so is he. Then I was thinking of how that the first beginning of the meetings, after I got started out, some of the events that happened, how people would have dreams and come to the meeting; how a great revival was in its making and stirring. And now it's just whirled out, went all around the world and everywhere. And tonight, even many of the Full Gospel churches are condemning divine healing and saying, "There is no such a thing. It's witchcraft and everything."
People who ought to be standing up for the powers of God. But you know what God does? He just sets that organization on the shelf and moves on just the same. Doesn't stop Him a bit. It's always.
All organizations will finally wind up over there in Babylon. Just like the Bible says they will. God out of the whole bunch… The very word church means "called out." "Come out from among them, my people, that you be not partakers of their sins." That's exactly right.
text_no:
I dag tenkte jeg. Som en mann tenker i sitt hjerte, slik er han også. Jeg reflekterte over starten av møtene, etter at jeg begynte, og noen av hendelsene som fant sted. Folk hadde drømmer og kom til møtene; en stor vekkelse var i emning og spredde seg. Nå har den ekspandert, spredt seg over hele verden. I kveld fordømmer til og med mange av Full Evangelium-menighetene guddommelig helbredelse og sier: "Det finnes ikke. Det er trolldom og alt mulig annet."
Personer som burde stå opp for Guds kraft. Men vet du hva Gud gjør? Han setter bare den organisasjonen på hyllen og fortsetter likevel. Det stopper Ham ikke det minste. Slik er det alltid.
Alle organisasjoner vil til slutt ende opp i Babylon, akkurat som Bibelen sier. Gud kaller sitt folk ut av mengden ... Selve ordet "menighet" betyr "utkalt." "Kom ut fra dem, Mitt folk, så dere ikke deltar i deres synder." Det er helt riktig.

text:
Now, I remember one time in Jonesboro, Arkansas, is one of the thrilling things that just come into my mind now. It happened on a night… I was having services at the auditorium, and there was so many people had gathered out there till the paper said we had twenty-eight thousand.
Well, if the paper said so, they were all there. Every one of them. And so they was for forty miles near Jonesboro. You couldn't even get a farm house. They had tent cities and everything, to take care of the people, for the accommodations.
When they began to see the marvel of God's great power to reveal things… It was all new. They just went right out. But now they seen it so much till, "Well, Lord, if that's all you got, well, I'll wait for something else." Oh, my! That's people, isn't it? That's people.
But I remember I hadn't seen my wife for about two months or three. And I had a little girl, my first little girl, which is… They'll be on the road up here tomorrow. She's seven years old now. She was just a little bitty fellow when I left home.
And when I come back, she didn't even know me. I'd lost most of my hair and I'd fell off a lot of weight, about twenty pounds, and stoop shoulders. And her mother would tell her, say, "That's daddy." My picture on the dresser. Said, "That's daddy."
text_no:
Jeg husker en gang i Jonesboro, Arkansas, som er en av de mest spennende opplevelsene jeg kan minnes. Det skjedde en kveld da jeg hadde møter i auditoriet. Så mange mennesker hadde samlet seg der ute at avisen meldte om tjueåtte tusen til stede.
Hvis avisen sa det, var de der, hver eneste en. Folk hadde kommet fra opptil førti mil unna Jonesboro, og det var ikke én gård som hadde ledige rom. De hadde satt opp teltbyer og alt mulig for å ta hånd om alle de tilreisende.
Da folk begynte å se Guds store kraft åpenbare ting, ble det en helt ny opplevelse for dem. De lot seg rive med. Men nå har de sett det så mange ganger at de tenker: "Vel, Herre, hvis det er alt Du har, så venter jeg på noe annet." Å, du store! Slik er folk, er det ikke? Slik er folk.
Jeg husker at jeg ikke hadde sett min kone på omtrent to eller tre måneder. Vi hadde en liten datter, vår første, som vil være her på veien i morgen. Hun er syv år nå. Da jeg dro hjemmefra, var hun bare en liten tass.
Da jeg kom tilbake, kjente hun meg ikke igjen. Jeg hadde mistet mesteparten av håret, gått ned i vekt, omtrent tjue pund, og fått krum rygg. Moren hennes pleide å vise henne et bilde på kommoden og si: "Det er pappa."

text:
And when I come in one cold day and walked into the place. We lived in a little two rooms. And my wife was in there, and we was talking. And I said, "Where's the baby?" Said, "Asleep in the next room."
So I went in to the little old crib. And I never will forget. I woke her up. And those big blue eyes looked at me, you know, and she was scared of me. That hurt me. And Mother picked her up and handed her over to me. She said, "That's daddy." She got back. That wasn't her daddy. She'd look over at the picture; didn't look like her daddy.
So, well, look, friends, I was preaching the Gospel. I was doing the best that I knowed how; as I'm doing tonight. Some glorious day it will be different over on the other side when we all get home.
And I had broke down. I was staying day and night in the platform. Stayed one time for about four days and nights or more, without even leaving the platform, praying for the sick. And there were more people at the end of the meeting, many times more, than there was when we started. And just no end to it, praying for the sick.
text_no:
En kald dag kom jeg inn og gikk mot stedet. Vi bodde i to små rom. Min kone var der inne, og vi snakket sammen. Jeg spurte: "Hvor er babyen?" Hun svarte: "Sover i det neste rommet."
Så jeg gikk inn til den lille, gamle barnesengen. Jeg vil aldri glemme det. Jeg vekket henne, og de store, blå øynene så på meg, og hun var redd for meg. Det gjorde vondt. Moren plukket henne opp og ga henne til meg. Hun sa: "Det er pappa." Men det var ikke pappaen hennes. Hun så på bildet; det lignet ikke på pappaen hennes.
Se, venner, jeg forkynte evangeliet. Jeg gjorde mitt beste, akkurat som jeg gjør i kveld. En herlig dag vil det være annerledes på den andre siden når vi alle kommer hjem.
Jeg hadde brutt sammen. Jeg oppholdt meg dag og natt på plattformen, én gang i fire døgn eller mer, uten å forlate plattformen, og ba for de syke. Det var flere folk ved slutten av møtene, mange ganger flere, enn det var da vi startet. Det var ingen ende på det, å be for de syke.

text:
And great things were taking place. I remember one night there, there was a lady came up, she had her handkerchief like this. I thought she was crying. It was about three or four o'clock in the morning. And just as soon as I took a hold of her hand and was talking to her… I thought she was weeping.
I said, "Oh, lady, you got cancer---haven't you?---on the nose? And she pulled the handkerchief back, and her whole nose was eaten off. And I said, "Do you believe that Jesus will heal you?"
And she said, "I have just left Memphis." And she said, "The doctors told me over there the other day, there was no hope for me now. It'd done got such a straight … start, rather, till the radium or nothing would stop it."
text_no:
Store ting skjedde. Jeg husker en natt der en kvinne kom opp til meg med et lommetørkle slik. Jeg trodde hun gråt. Det var rundt klokken tre eller fire om morgenen. Så snart jeg tok hånden hennes og snakket med henne, trodde jeg hun gråt.
Jeg sa: "Å kjære kvinne, du har kreft, har du ikke? På nesen?" Hun trakk lommetørkleet tilbake, og hele nesen hennes var fortært. Jeg sa: "Tror du at Jesus vil helbrede deg?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg har nettopp forlatt Memphis. Legene fortalte meg der forleden dag at det ikke var noe håp for meg nå. Det hadde utviklet seg så mye at verken radium eller noe annet ville stoppe det."

text:
And oh, I thought, That sure is too bad. And just a little bit after that, why, I prayed for her. And about three or four months from then, I was over at Texarkana, and was in a little meeting there.
And the first thing you know, I seen somebody waving at me back there, and waving at me, and I didn't know who it was. This young fellow jumped up, he was an exterminator there, and he raised up.
He said, "Brother Branham, I just can't hold it any longer."
I said, "What's the matter, son?"
And he said, "This is my mother. Do you recognize her?"
I said, "No, I don't."
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Det var synd." Kort etter ba jeg for henne. Tre eller fire måneder senere var jeg i Texarkana på et lite møte.
Plutselig så jeg noen vinke til meg bak i rommet, og jeg visste ikke hvem det var. En ung mann reiste seg og ropte: "Bror Branham, jeg klarer ikke å holde det inne lenger."
Jeg spurte, "Hva er i veien, sønn?"
Han svarte, "Dette er min mor. Kjenner du henne igjen?"
Jeg sa, "Nei, det gjør jeg ikke."

text:
She said, "I was the woman who didn't have any nose about three months ago." She had another nose. God had healed her. It had growed back. And I have her name and address and her testimony.
And the trouble … the thing was, she was doctoring, she went back to her doctor. And he said, "Oh, my!"
She said, "What did it look like now?"
Said, "Why, say." Said, "What's happened?"
She said, "I changed doctors."
Said, "Well, who did you go to?"
She said, "I went to Dr. Jesus."
He said, "Dr. Jesus?" Said, "Let's see. Where does He practice at?" Said, "I don't believe…" My! Isn't that something? Oh, my!
But many things happen along. I remember another night there that something taken place. I was at Little Rock and there was in a Rev. Mr. Brown… If you'd like for this testimony, just write Reverend G. H. Brown, 505 Victor Street. V-i-c-t-o-r, Victor Street, in Little Rock, Arkansas.
text_no:
Hun sa: "Jeg var kvinnen uten nese for omtrent tre måneder siden." Hun hadde fått en ny nese. Gud hadde helbredet henne, og nesen hadde vokst tilbake. Jeg har hennes navn, adresse og vitnesbyrd.
Problemet var at hun tidligere hadde vært i behandling, og hun dro tilbake til legen sin. Han utbrøt: "Å, min!"
Hun spurte: "Hvordan ser det ut nå?"
Han sa: "Vel, hva?" og fortsatte: "Hva har skjedd?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg byttet lege."
Han spurte: "Vel, hvem gikk du til da?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg gikk til Dr. Jesus."
Han sa: "Dr. Jesus? La oss se. Hvor praktiserer Han? Jeg tror ikke..." Min Gud! Er ikke det noe?
Det skjer mange slike ting. Jeg husker en annen kveld hvor noe annet skjedde. Jeg var i Little Rock, hos pastor Brown. Hvis du ønsker dette vitnesbyrdet, skriver du til Reverend G. H. Brown, 505 Victor Street, V-i-c-t-o-r, Victor Street, i Little Rock, Arkansas.

text:
We'd been praying. And, oh, the newspapers had give me the awfullest write-up, saying that I'd blocked… Had the ambulances out there. Said, "A self-style, nattily-dressed Baptist preacher." Oh, you never heard such in your life.
And they had the streetcars blocked off in the streets from the ambulances. And I'd been praying for about two or three hours.
And Rev. Brown come to me (which is one of the sponsors on the meeting), he said, "Brother Branham, when you get finished, down at the bottom of the auditorium," he said, "there's a case like you've never seen before."
I'd been praying for the sick then about three or four months. Seen many things in that much time. And he said, "When you get a chance to take a little breath…" I was going to pray for them all night anyhow. Said, "Come down. I want to show you something like you've never seen before."
I said, "All right." So after awhile I went down to the steps. And now, friends, when I got down…
text_no:
Vi hadde bedt, og avisene hadde gitt meg den verste omtalen, og hevdet at jeg hadde blokkert gatene og forårsaket kaos med ambulanser. De beskrev meg som en "selvutnevnt, elegant kledd baptistpredikant." Du har aldri hørt maken i ditt liv.
Ambulanser stengte trikkene i gatene, og jeg hadde bedt i to til tre timer. 
Rev. Brown, en av sponsorene for møtet, kom til meg og sa: "Bror Branham, når du er ferdig, nede ved bunnen av auditoriet, er det et tilfelle du aldri har sett maken til."
På dette tidspunktet hadde jeg bedt for de syke i omtrent tre til fire måneder og hadde sett mye. Han sa: "Når du får sjansen til å puste litt… Jeg skulle uansett be for dem hele natten. Kom ned, jeg vil vise deg noe du aldri har sett før."
Jeg svarte: "Greit." Så etter en stund gikk jeg ned trappene. Og nå, venner, når jeg kom ned…

text:
I'm just telling these things for God's glory, you see. For the glory of Jesus Christ, that you might know some of the things that's happened. Oh, millions of things … oh, I wouldn't say millions, but many thousands of things, anyhow, has happened.
And I went down at the bottom of the steps. And there laid a lady, laying on the floor. She was dressed with like shorts. And she had both hands sticking up like this and her feet sticking right up. Laying on her back. Just laying there like that.
I said… There was a typical old Arkansas brother standing there, and he…
I said, "Is that your wife?"
He said, "It is, Brother Branham."
I said, "Why is her legs bleeding like they are?"
Said, "Well…" Said, "She's from the insane institution." Said, "She was going to the time of menopause. And she had a baby born and it threw her into a premature menopause. And the doctor give her some shots and it went to her head, and so she went wild."
And she'd been in the insane institution two years. And he'd heard about (from over in Mississippi) a soldier's mother that had been healed and taken out of the institution. So, he said, "I brought her."
text_no:
Jeg forteller disse tingene for Guds ære, skjønner du. For Jesu Kristi ære, slik at du kan vite om noen av de tingene som har skjedd. Å, millioner av ting ... kanskje ikke millioner, men mange tusen ting har i alle fall skjedd.
Jeg gikk ned trappene. Der lå en dame på gulvet, kledd i noe som lignet shorts. Hun hadde begge hendene og føttene strukket rett opp, liggende på ryggen.
Jeg sa ... Der sto en typisk gammel Arkansas-bror, og han ...
Jeg spurte, "Er det din kone?"
Han svarte, "Ja, Bror Branham."
Jeg spurte, "Hvorfor blør bena hennes slik?"
Han sa, "Vel ... Hun kommer fra et mentalsykehus. Hun var i overgangsalderen. Hun hadde født en baby, og dette kastet henne inn i en for tidlig overgangsalder. Legen ga henne noen sprøyter, noe som påvirket hodet hennes, og hun ble gal."
Hun hadde vært på mentalsykehuset i to år. Han hadde hørt om en soldats mor fra Mississippi som hadde blitt helbredet og tatt ut av institusjonen. Så han sa, "Jeg tok henne med hit."

text:
Said, "Brother Branham, I've got four little children at home." And he said, "And we've sold our farm and everything," he said, "to try to get her well. And she's taking treatments."
And said, "Then today," he said, "I sold my mules." He said, "I had to bring her down here." And said, "They let me bring her out, just over the night." And said, "They couldn't get her in an ambulance. And I'd taken some brothers up there. And five of us got her out of the place and put her in a car." And said, "That's where she kicked all the glasses out of the car coming down. Where five men couldn't hold her."
And when they got her in the building there. Brother Brown said she'd just taken the place. She'd just throw men every way. So, when they'd let her down, though, she'd lay on her back and just throw her hands and feet up like that. Her eyes set real glassy. And that's the way she stayed.
Why, I said, "That's too bad." I said, "I'll walk out and take a hold of her hand."
He said, "Oh, Brother Branham, don't you do that." Said, "She'd kill you."
Oh, I said, "I don't think so." And just a boy like on the way. So, not having too much dealing with demonology.
So, he said, "Brother Branham, I warn you. Don't do that."
text_no:
Han sa, "Bror Branham, jeg har fire små barn hjemme." Han fortsatte, "Vi har solgt gården og alt vi eier for å forsøke å få henne frisk. Hun er under behandling."
Han sa videre, "I dag solgte jeg muldyrene mine. Jeg måtte ta henne med hit. De lot meg ta henne ut over natten, men vi kunne ikke få henne inn i en ambulanse. Noen brødre og jeg klarte å få henne ut av stedet og plassert i en bil." Han sa, "Hun sparket ut alle bilrutene på vei hit, og fem menn klarte ikke å holde henne."
Da de fikk henne inn i bygningen, sa Bror Brown at hun kastet menn hit og dit. Når de la henne ned, lå hun på ryggen og kastet hendene og føttene i været. Øynene hennes var blanke og glassaktige, og slik lå hun stille.
Jeg sa, "Det var leit." Jeg fortsatte, "Jeg går ut og tar henne i hånden."
Han svarte, "Å, Bror Branham, ikke gjør det. Hun vil drepe deg."
Jeg sa, "Jeg tror ikke det," og som en ung gutt uten mye erfaring med demonologi, var jeg ikke bekymret.
Han advarte igjen, "Bror Branham, jeg advarer deg. Ikke gjør det."

text:
And I said, "Oh, I don't think she'd hurt me, brother." And I walked down a little step like that, walked out on the floor. She was laying there with her hands up. I said, "How do you do, sister?" Took a hold of her hand. And just as I took hold of her hand, she just gripped down like that.
And she gave a pull like that. And she pulled me practically off of my feet. And she would have threw me across the room. Why, she would have weighed about a hundred, oh, I guess seventy or eighty pounds, and that demon power.
Did you ever see a person possessed with demon power? They got four times their strength. Well, if the devil can give you four times strength when possessed with him, what about possessed with the Holy Ghost? How much power could God give you, see?
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Å, jeg tror ikke hun vil skade meg, Bror." Jeg gikk ned et lite trappetrinn og ut på gulvet. Hun lå der med hendene opp. Jeg sa: "Hvordan har du det, Søster?" og tok hånden hennes. I det øyeblikket jeg tok hånden hennes, grep hun hardt.
Hun tok tak og dro til. Hun trakk meg nesten av føttene, og hun kunne ha kastet meg over rommet. Hun veide kanskje rundt hundre og sytti eller åtti pund, og den demoniske kraften...
Har du noen gang sett en person besatt av demonisk kraft? De får fire ganger sin normale styrke. Vel, hvis djevelen kan gi deg firedobbel styrke når du er besatt av ham, hvor mye kraft kan ikke Gud gi deg når du er besatt av Den Hellige Ånd?

text:
That's the reason you're afraid, you people that's crippled. Don't be afraid! Fling away them crutches, and get up and start off. God's with you. If He's anointed you, He'll give it to you. That's right. Be anointed with the Holy Spirit.
And then, when this woman, she jerked me like that. And I almost come off of my feet. And I threw my foot like that and caught her right across the breast like this. I caught … and it pulled my hand out of hers.
Well, I ran back towards the steps again. He said, "I told you, Brother Branham." And she started making a real funny noise, going "Hee-hee, hmm-hmm-hmm." And she started crawling like a snake on her back. Just a real… You could hear her dragging, you know. Just drag, drag, like that across the floor, concrete, like that.
And she got against the wall, put her great big strong limbs against the wall, and kicked like that. And when she did, she hit a seat and her head hit like that. There's a seat sitting there where the people had been sitting in before they brought her there.
text_no:
Derfor er dere redde, dere som er lamme. Ikke vær redde! Kast bort krykkene og reis dere. Gud er med dere. Hvis Han har salvet dere, vil Han gi det til dere. Det er riktig. Bli salvet med Den Hellige Ånd.
Så, da denne kvinnen rykket til meg slik. Jeg holdt nesten på å miste balansen. Jeg kastet foten slik og traff henne rett over brystet. Jeg trakk ut hånden fra hennes grep.
Vel, jeg løp tilbake mot trappene. Han sa: "Jeg sa det til deg, Bror Branham." Hun begynte å lage en merkelig lyd, som "Hee-hee, hmm-hmm-hmm", og begynte å krype som en slange på ryggen. Bare helt... Man kunne høre henne dra seg bortover gulvet, betonggulvet.
Hun kom bort til veggen, satte de sterke armene sine mot den og sparket. Da hun gjorde det, traff hun et sete og hodet traff det. Det var et sete der folk hadde sittet før de førte henne inn.

text:
And it broke the seat … the bench, it was. And the hair and blood come out of her head. She just started making real funny noise. Picked up that thing in her hand like that and threw it at her husband. And just knocked the plaster off the wall where it hit right by the side of where he was at.
Said, "See, Brother Branham?" Said, "Now all hopes is gone." Said, "What can I do?" And the poor old fellow started crying, put his arms up around me like that---old patched overalls on. I felt so sorry for the boy.
And I said, "Well, brother… Look, the only thing I know to tell you, that this Angel that met me told me that if I'd be sincere and get the people to believe, when I prayed, that He'd heal the sick." I said, "Do you believe that?"
The simple faith to believe that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, cast out devils. And He has gone up in to the Father now, and He's here in the form of the Holy Spirit. I said, "I believe He's the same Jesus today that He was then." I said, "I don't have to tell you what's wrong. You know what's wrong."
He said, "That's right."
text_no:
Benken knakk... det var en benk. Hår og blod strømmet ut fra hodet hennes. Hun begynte å lage rare lyder og plukket opp noe i hånden sin, kastet det mot ektemannen. Det traff veggen ved siden av ham og slo av en del av pussen.
"Ser du, Bror Branham?" sa han. "Nå er alle håp borte. Hva kan jeg gjøre?" Den stakkars gamle mannen begynte å gråte, omfavnet meg med sine gjennomhullede overaller. Jeg følte så med ham.
Jeg sa, "Vel, bror... Se, det eneste jeg kan fortelle deg er at Engelen som møtte meg, sa at hvis jeg var oppriktig og fikk folk til å tro når jeg ba, ville Han helbrede de syke." Jeg spurte, "Tror du det?"
Den enkle troen på at Jesus Kristus, Guds Sønn, driver ut onde ånder. Han har nå gått til Faderen og er her i form av Den Hellige Ånd. Jeg sa, "Jeg tror Han er den samme Jesus i dag som Han var dengang." Jeg sa, "Jeg trenger ikke å fortelle deg hva som er galt. Du vet hva som er galt."
"Det stemmer," sa han.

text:
I said, "Do you believe if I'll ask God, God'll do it?"
He said, "I do." And about that time, she turned around on her back.
She said, "William Branham, you haven't got nothing to do with me. I brought her here." Come crawling up towards me.
Why, her husband turned, said, "Well, what's this?" Said, "That woman don't even know her own name." Said, "She hasn't spoke a word in two years."
I said, "That wasn't her. That was that devil. See, that's that devil. He knows that he's going to have to leave the woman if you will only believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, right now."
And he kept calling like that. "You have nothing to do with me. I brought her here."
So, I said, "Now, you agree with me."
He said, "All right."
I said, "Heavenly Father, in the name of Your Son Jesus Christ, make that devil leave the woman."
So, she never talked no more. He said, "What shall I do?"
I said, "Take her on back after service is over. If you believe, it's got to happen."
He said, "All right."
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Tror du at Gud vil gjøre det hvis jeg ber Ham om det?"
Han svarte: "Det gjør jeg." Og i det øyeblikket snudde hun seg på ryggen.
Hun sa: "William Branham, du har ingenting med meg å gjøre. Jeg brakte henne hit." Hun kom krypende mot meg.
Hennes ektemann ble forundret og sa: "Hva er dette? Den kvinnen kjenner ikke engang sitt eget navn. Hun har ikke sagt et ord på to år."
Jeg sa: "Det var ikke henne. Det var den djevelen. Han vet at han må forlate kvinnen hvis du bare vil tro på Herren Jesus Kristus akkurat nå."
Den stemmen fortsatte å si: "Du har ingenting med meg å gjøre. Jeg brakte henne hit."
Så sa jeg: "Nå må du være enig med meg."
Han svarte: "Greit."
Jeg sa: "Himmelske Far, i Din Sønn Jesu Kristi navn, få den djevelen til å forlate kvinnen."
Hun snakket ikke mer. Han spurte: "Hva skal jeg gjøre nå?"
Jeg sa: "Ta henne med tilbake etter at møtet er over. Hvis du tror, må det skje."
Han sa: "Greit."

text:
So, about three months after that, I was up here at Jonesboro. And I happened to see somebody back there waving. And I didn't know who it was, sitting right out like that. And they was waving at me. And it was the woman sitting there.
She said, "Don't you recognize me, Brother Branham?"
I said, "No, I don't." She had her four little children all sitting around.
She said, "The last time you seen me, they told me that I was bleeding all over and I was out of my head."
And he had took the woman back. Had no trouble taking her back, she sat up. And the second day, when she was in the institution… I said, "Let them dismiss her."
So, the second day, they come to her and she was sitting up. The third day they give her a discharge from the place, sent her home in her right mind, see. That's right. Jesus Christ, the same yesterday, today, and forever.
In that night… I have just about time for about another little short testimony.
text_no:
Omtrent tre måneder etter det, var jeg her i Jonesboro. Jeg så noen vinke der bak, men jeg visste ikke hvem det var. Det var en kvinne.
Hun sa: "Kjenner du meg ikke igjen, Bror Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, det gjør jeg ikke." Hun hadde fire små barn rundt seg.
Hun sa: "Sist gang du så meg, sa de at jeg blødde kraftig og var fra sans og samling."
Mannen hennes hadde tatt henne med tilbake. Det var ingen problemer med å ta henne med tilbake, hun satt oppreist. Den andre dagen, da hun var på institusjonen, sa jeg: "La dem utskrive henne."
Så, den andre dagen, kom de til henne, og hun satt oppreist. Den tredje dagen ga de henne utskrivelse fra stedet og sendte henne hjem i sin rette mentale tilstand. Det stemmer. Jesus Kristus, Den samme i går, i dag og for alltid.
Den kvelden har jeg tid til et lite vitnesbyrd til.

text:
My wife had never seen one of the meetings. And I sent for her to come down. I was so lonesome. And so, she came down. And Brother Reed… This was at Jonesboro. And T. Richard Reed could give you the testimony of this.
The Blessed Old Bible Hour Tabernacle; he's the pastor of it down in Jonesboro, Arkansas, if you'd like to get this testimony.
And then, I remember that night when we started the meeting, for three city blocks standing in the street, you couldn't… The cops was there. Just letting them as they would pile up like that and block off the sides. And keep up.
And my wife said, "Did all these people come to hear you preach, honey?"
I said, "No, honey. They come to see Jesus." And the little fellow stood there. She's very backward, holding my hand, crying. And she sang this song:
Oh, they come from the East and West,
They come from the land afar,
(You've heard it.)
To feast with the King, to dine as His guest.
How happy these pilgrims are!
text_no:
Min kone hadde aldri deltatt på ett av møtene. Jeg inviterte henne til å komme ned fordi jeg følte meg så ensom. Så hun kom. Og Bror Reed... Dette var i Jonesboro. T. Richard Reed kan vitne om dette. Han er pastor ved The Blessed Old Bible Hour Tabernacle i Jonesboro, Arkansas. Hvis du ønsker å få hans vitnesbyrd, kan du kontakte ham der.
Jeg husker den kvelden da møtet startet. Tre kvartaler i byen var gaten full av folk. Politiet var til stede og forsøkte å holde orden, men mengden samlet seg og blokkerte sidene. 
Min kone spurte, "Er alle disse menneskene kommet for å høre deg forkynne, kjære?"
Jeg svarte, "Nei, kjære. De kom for å se Jesus." Hun stod der, tilbakeholden og holdt hånden min mens hun gråt. Så sang hun denne sangen:
Å, de kommer fra øst og vest,
De kommer fra fjerne land,
(du har hørt den)
For å gjeste Kongen, være Hans gjest.
Hvor lykkelige disse pilegrimene er!

text:
And she was standing there singing that for me. And then the ushers come forth and said, "Brother Branham, they'd been waiting." And so down… I never knowed what become of her.
And they got me into the building. And just as I got in there, I happened to look down. Oh, the ambulance stretchers and everything laying. There's two nurses by a little girl here. She had TB.
And I could just feel that something was going to take place right there. And while I was watching it, there was a man standing over on this side with a blue uniform, kept waving his hat.
I said, "Are you trying to talk to me, sir?"
He said, "Yes, sir."
Said, "I've got a case out here that she's dying. And I think she's already dead." Said, "I can't find a doctor anywhere." And said, "We had a doctor there, just a house doctor." And said, "I think she's already dead." And said, "Would you come to her?"
I said, "Brother, there's probably two thousand people back in there." I said, "I don't believe I could do it."
And four or five ushers stepped down and said, "Well, we'll help you get up there."
We started out. Got out to the ambulance where… Oh, there was about eight or ten ambulances setting in the row. And this is a big ambulance.
text_no:
Hun sto der og sang for meg. Så kom vaktene frem og sa: "Bror Branham, de har ventet." Så nedover… Jeg vet aldri hva som skjedde med henne etterpå.
De fikk meg inn i bygningen. Akkurat da jeg kom inn, så jeg ned. Åh, ambulansebårene og alt annet lå overalt. To sykepleiere stod ved en liten jente. Hun hadde tuberkulose.
Jeg kunne bare føle at noe kom til å skje der og da. Mens jeg så på, var det en mann stående på denne siden i en blå uniform, som fortsatte å vinke med hatten sin.
Jeg sa: "Prøver du å snakke med meg, sir?"
Han svarte: "Ja, sir. Jeg har et tilfelle her ute hvor hun holder på å dø, og jeg tror hun allerede er død." Han fortsatte: "Jeg kan ikke finne en lege noe sted. Vi hadde en lege, bare en huslege, men jeg tror hun allerede er død. Kan du komme til henne?"
Jeg sa: "Bror, det er sannsynligvis to tusen mennesker her inne. Jeg tror ikke jeg kan gjøre det."
Fire eller fem vakter trådte frem og sa: "Vi hjelper deg med å komme dit."
Vi begynte å gå. Kom frem til ambulansen der… Åh, det var omtrent åtte eller ti ambulanser på rad. Dette var en stor ambulanse.

text:
The man told me said, "There's been a man had been healed that morning, blind---been blind for ten years. And he was prayed for, told him he was going to receive his sight. And on the road home that morning, his eyes come open." He was riding an old Model-T Ford up them rough roads in Arkansas. And he begin to scream. They stopped the car and out and around and around the car he went screaming. He run into the city, Kennett or some little place down in Missouri, that little boot-hill type of Missouri there.
And he run and went into the church, the Catholic church, and begin to testify and they throwed him out. And he had his hat hanging on his black cane like … or, his white cane, rather, his black hat, going down the street screaming to the top of his voice. Used to be a shoe cobbler there.
And he was going down the street screaming and praising God. He went into the Methodist church. They throwed him out of there. They didn't know… He was taking the city. Now, I tell you, he was… And they had everybody in the hospital wanting to come down and be prayed for.
So, the ambulance said, "I've got to go back." And said, "I've got to…" So we got in there and when…
I said, "Well, now you just open the door." We went in and a very typical old dad there, his old hat sewed with twine, wrapping string, you know, around.
text_no:
Mannen fortalte meg: "Det var en mann som ble helbredet den morgenen. Han hadde vært blind i ti år. Etter å ha blitt bedt for, ble han fortalt at han skulle få synet tilbake. På vei hjem den morgenen åpnet øynene hans seg." Han kjørte en gammel Model-T Ford på de grove veiene i Arkansas. Han begynte å skrike. De stoppet bilen, og han løp rundt bilen mens han skrek. Så løp han inn i byen, Kennett eller en liten plass i Missouri, i det lille boot-hill-området der.
Han løp inn i den katolske kirken og begynte å vitne, men de kastet ham ut. Han hadde hatten hengende på stokken sin – en hvit stokk med en svart hatt. Han gikk nedover gaten og ropte høyt. Han pleide å være skomaker der.
Mens han skrek og priste Gud, gikk han inn i metodistkirken. De kastet ham ut der også. De visste ikke hva de skulle gjøre. Han skapte oppstandelse i byen. Jeg forteller deg, alle på sykehuset ville komme ned for å bli bedt for.
Ambulansesjåføren sa: "Jeg må tilbake." Så vi kom dit, og jeg sa: "Bare åpne døren." Vi gikk inn til en eldre, typisk far med en gammel hatt sydd med hyssing.

text:
Those Arkansas people are poor as they can be. But, brother, under them old blue shirts beats some real good true Holy Ghost-filled hearts. Yes, sir. I'd rather have one of them with me any time, than maybe a slicker with his collar turned around in the back. And I couldn't trust him out of my sight. Yes, indeedy. Yes, indeed.
Just, I tell this old time religion, brother, it straightens you up. That's right. It will make a silk dress and a calico put their arms around one another and call "sister." Is that right? It will make a tuxedo suit and a pair of overalls call each other "brother." That's what it'll do, brother.
It'll clean you up. It'll make no difference in you. It tears down that self, and starch, and pride, that we've got.
text_no:
De folkene i Arkansas er så fattige som de kan være. Men, Bror, under de gamle blå skjortene banker noen virkelig gode, sanne, Åndsfylte hjerter. Ja, sir. Jeg ville heller hatt en av dem med meg når som helst, enn en glatt fyr med snippen på vrangen som jeg ikke kunne stole på utenfor synsvidde. Ja, absolutt.
Jeg forteller deg, denne gammeldagse religionen, Bror, den retter deg opp. Det stemmer. Den vil få en silkekjole og en bomullskjole til å legge armene rundt hverandre og kalle hverandre "søster." Er det ikke sant? Den vil få en smoking og en kjeledress til å kalle hverandre "bror." Det er det den gjør, Bror.
Den vil rense deg. Den vil ikke gjøre forskjell på deg. Den bryter ned egoet, stivheten og stoltheten som vi har.

text:
And, so then I remember getting in there and he had the soles of his shoes was out. He was kneeling down hollering, "O God, give her back to me, God." Poor old fellow. I happened to think of my own old dad when I see him gripping that hat like that.
I said, "What's the matter, dad?"
He said, "Who are you?"
And I said, "I'm Brother Branham."
He said, "Oh, Brother Branham," said, "mother's such a sweet woman." And said, "I've lost her I'm sure."
text_no:
Jeg husker at jeg gikk inn der og merket at sålene på skoene hans var utslitt. Han knelte og ropte: "O Gud, gi henne tilbake til meg, Gud." Stakkars gamle mann. Jeg tenkte på min egen gamle far da jeg så ham gripe hatten slik.
Jeg spurte: "Hva er i veien, far?"
Han svarte: "Hvem er du?"
Jeg sa: "Jeg er Bror Branham."
Han utbrøt: "Å, Bror Branham, mor er en så søt kvinne. Jeg er sikker på at jeg har mistet henne."

text:
And I said, "Well, what's the matter?" So I took a hold of her hand and her eyes were set now. But I do not think she was dead, see. And her false teeth had been taken out. And laying down. Like muddy-looking water had come down from her eyes. And her eyes were set right back. Real old woman. Not real old, but past the middle age; I'd say sixty-eight, seventy, somewhere along there.
So she was laying like that. And I said… And I felt of her. Her forehead was sticky with perspiration. And he said, "She just quit breathing a while ago, Brother Branham." Said, "Oh, she's a good woman." Said, "She's hired over them old clods and helped to make me a living." Said, "We raised a bunch of children." And said, "She took this cancer." And said, "The doctor has worked faithfully. He's done all he could do." It was in the female glands. And said, "There's nothing more he could do." And said, "We sold her quilts to get the ambulance to bring us down here." The quilts that she had made.
text_no:
Jeg spurte: "Hva er galt?" Jeg tok tak i hånden hennes, og øynene hennes var nå stive. Jeg tror ikke hun var død. Hun hadde tatt ut de falske tennene sine. Det så ut som grumsete vann hadde rent fra øynene hennes, som var stive og tilbaketrukne. Hun var en eldre kvinne, ikke veldig gammel, men litt over middelaldrende; jeg vil anslå rundt sekstifem til sytti år.
Hun lå slik, og jeg kjente på henne. Pannen hennes var klam av svette. Han sa: "Hun sluttet å puste for en liten stund siden, Bror Branham. Hun var en god kvinne. Hun har slitt hardt og hjulpet meg med å forsørge oss. Vi har oppdratt en flokk barn sammen. Hun fikk kreft, og legen har gjort sitt beste. Det var i de kvinnelige kjertlene, og legen kunne ikke gjøre mer. Vi solgte teppene hennes for å få råd til ambulansen som brakte oss hit." Disse teppene hadde hun laget selv.

text:
I thought, O God. I said, "Well let's pray, brother." And I took a hold of her hand. And I said, "Heavenly Father…" I was going to pray for consolation. I'll confess it. I thought the woman was gone.
And so, while I was praying I felt something grip my hand. The devil said to me said, "That's just the flesh you know. She's dead and that's just the nerves twitching." And I went ahead praying a little bit longer. I felt a twitch again. I looked. I kept praying, but I was looking over at her.
And the old man was down on the floor crying, "God, give me back mother." And I was holding her hands like that. And then I happened to look again. And I seen the skin on her forehead wrinkling.
And she looked up at me. She said, "What's your name?"
And I said, "I'm Brother Branham."
She said, "Well, we come down for you to pray for me."
And I said, "Yes, ma'am."
She said, "I feel so good."
I said, "Would you set up?"
And about that time he looked, the old man looked at me hollered, "Mother, mother, mother!" The tears running down his poor old whiskered cheeks. And he grabbed her like that and they began to hug and kissing one another.
And oh, three or four months later, she was in the meeting down there testifying, just shelling the woods with that testimony.
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "O Gud." Jeg sa, "La oss be, Bror." Og jeg tok hånden hennes. Jeg sa, "Himmelske Far…" Jeg skulle be for trøst. Jeg må innrømme det; jeg trodde kvinnen var borte. 
Mens jeg ba, følte jeg noe gripe hånden min. Djevelen sa til meg, "Det er bare kroppen. Hun er død, og det er bare nervene som ryker." Jeg fortsatte å be litt lenger. Jeg følte en rykning igjen. Jeg holdt øynene åpne mens jeg ba og kikket bort på henne. 
Den gamle mannen lå på gulvet og gråt, "Gud, gi meg min kone tilbake." Jeg holdt hendene hennes fast. Da jeg kikket opp igjen, så jeg rynker på pannen hennes. 
Hun så på meg og spurte, "Hva heter du?" 
Jeg svarte, "Jeg er Bror Branham." 
Hun sa, "Vi kom hit for at du skulle be for meg." 
Jeg svarte, "Ja, frue."
Hun sa, "Jeg føler meg så bra." 
Jeg sa, "Kan du sette deg opp?" 
På det tidspunktet så den gamle mannen på meg og ropte, "Kjære, kjære!" Tårer rant nedover hans gamle, skjeggete kinn. Han grep tak i henne, og de begynte å omfavne og kysse hverandre. 
Tre eller fire måneder senere var hun på møtet der nede og vitnet, og delte sitt vitnesbyrd med stor kraft.

text:
So, I started to go out and the usher said, "Why Brother Branham, you couldn't get out." Said, "There's thousands of people that's banked in here."
And so, I said, "Well, if you'll just act like you're taking off your coat now. They won't notice me and I'll slip out this other side. And you send them ushers around. Nobody knows me back there in that parking lot." Just standing full of people and it raining. Just pouring right on down. They was standing out there anyhow. Holding papers over their children and things like that. Been laying on the streets for five or six days at a time like that. Laying under cotton trucks, anything they get. They love the Lord. They come to church. Brother, they love Him and they believe Him.
And then I went around the back of the place like that. I looked like a hypocrite to do that, but I just couldn't get in to where they was all at. And the poor things just almost stampede you. So, he started taking off his coat like this. And I slipped out the door and went up down through that ambulance row, come around in the back, and started in up there.
text_no:
Jeg begynte å gå ut, og vakten sa: "Broder Branham, du kan ikke komme ut." Han sa: "Det er tusenvis av mennesker her inne."
Så jeg sa: "Hvis du bare later som om du tar av deg frakken nå, vil de ikke legge merke til meg, og jeg kan snike meg ut på den andre siden. Send noen vakter rundt. Ingen kjenner meg der ute på parkeringsplassen." Det var fullt av folk, og det regnet kraftig. De stod der uansett, holdt aviser over barna sine og lignende. De hadde ligget på gatene i fem eller seks dager som dette, sov under bomullslastebiler eller hva de kunne finne. De elsker Herren. De kommer til Menigheten. Broder, de elsker Ham og tror på Ham.
Så gikk jeg rundt baksiden av stedet. Jeg følte meg som en hykler som gjorde det, men det var umulig å komme dit de alle var. De stakkars menneskene var i ferd med å storme mot meg. Så han begynte å ta av seg frakken, og jeg sneik meg ut døra, gikk ned gjennom radene av ambulanser, kom rundt på baksiden og begynte å gå inn derfra.

text:
Big flood lights was back in behind the place. And they had a bunch of chartered buses sitting like that. And I was going along through there, pushing along you know like this. And nobody knew me, nobody had seen me yet, because they couldn't get near the building.
They'd stayed right in that building. What was in there, kept their places. And so, they send someone out to get some sandwiches, or somebody hold their place while they had to got out and come back, you know. They was holding their place.
Then I was thinking as I went along there. Somebody kept saying, "Quit pushing. Quit pushing."
I said, "Excuse me." Just kept on you know pushing along. I pushed up beside of a great big tall fellow standing there whittling with his knife. And I pushed right under him like that.
He said, "Quit pushing."
I did because I was afraid he was going to push. So, I said, "Excuse me, sir."
text_no:
Store flomlys var plassert bak bygningen. Det sto en rekke leide busser parkert der. Jeg gikk gjennom området, dyttet meg fremover, som du vet. Ingen kjente meg igjen; ingen hadde sett meg ennå, fordi de ikke kunne komme nær bygningen.
Inne i bygningen holdt folk seg på plass. De sendte noen ut for å kjøpe smørbrød eller fikk noen til å holde plassen sin mens de gikk ut og kom tilbake. De passet godt på plassene sine.
Mens jeg beveget meg gjennom mengden, ropte noen stadig "Slutt å dytte! Slutt å dytte!"
Jeg sa, "Unnskyld," og fortsatte å presse meg fremover. Jeg kom opp ved siden av en stor, høy kar som sto og spikket med en kniv. Jeg dyttet meg helt opp under ham.
Han sa, "Slutt å dytte."
Jeg sluttet, redd for at han skulle dytte tilbake. Så jeg sa, “Unnskyld, sir.”

text:
And I stood with my hands down like this. He looked down at me kind of sarcastic like, you know, and kept on whittling. I said, "I'm sorry." He never said a word. Just kept going. I said, "Yes, sir." I watched and I thought, Well, I'll start pushing again in a few minutes when he gets to thinking about something else.
So I looked around. So after a while I heard somebody hollering, "Daddy! Daddy!" I wonder where that was. It sound like a southern voice of the colored people. Usually all the Southerners talk a whole lot alike. And I happened to look and coming out in the midst of people, more than there is sitting here, standing in that back parking lot, was a young colored girl.
Now, down there they have a law of segregation---Jim Crow law. And the white and colored cannot come together. And so… I don't think that's right. But so, that's wrong. I just don't say that for any respects for our colored friends here tonight, but I don't care if there is nobody here. That's wrong. That's right.
text_no:
Jeg sto med hendene nede. Han så ned på meg, litt sarkastisk, og fortsatte å spikke. Jeg sa: "Unnskyld." Han svarte ikke, bare fortsatte. Jeg sa: "Ja, herr." Jeg observerte ham og tenkte: Vel, jeg begynner å dytte igjen om noen minutter når han begynner å tenke på noe annet.
Jeg så meg rundt. Etter en stund hørte jeg noen rope: "Pappa! Pappa!" Jeg lurte på hvor det kom fra. Det hørtes ut som en sørstatsstemme fra de fargede. Vanligvis snakker alle fra sør omtrent på samme måte. Så jeg så meg rundt i folkemengden, som var større enn de som sitter her nå, og i bakerste parkeringsplass sto en ung farget jente.
Der nede har de en lov om segregering---Jim Crow-loven. Hvite og fargede kan ikke komme sammen. Og det mener jeg ikke er riktig. Det er feil. Jeg sier ikke dette av hensyn til våre fargede venner her i kveld, men selv om ingen andre var her, ville jeg fortsatt si at det er feil.

text:
And I'm a Southerner but that's still wrong. I'm a Abraham Lincoln man. I believe that God made us all the same. That's right.
And I seen the girl coming and I seen her eyes was as white with cataracts as my shirt. Nobody would help her. She was just pushing through the crowd hollering, "Daddy! Daddy!" like that.
And I thought, Poor old thing. And I thought, I believe I will get over to see what she wants. Nobody knows me. And I was watching for those ushers to come around the corner. They was trying to get through the crowd. So I pushed up close to where she was. And when she started this way, I'd get over this way, so I'd get right in front of her. And so that looked like awful to do that.
So she bumped right in to me. She said, "Pardon me."
I said, "Yes, ma'am."
She said, "Pardon me, sir." When she heard my voice of being a man.
And I said, "What's the matter?"
She said, "Sir, I've lost my daddy. I can't find him." Said, "I's blind. And I can't find my daddy."
And I said, "Well, where you from?"
She said, "I'm from Memphis."
text_no:
Jeg er fra Sørstatene, men det er fortsatt feil. Jeg er en Abraham Lincoln-tilhenger. Jeg tror at Gud skapte oss alle like. Det er riktig.
Jeg så en jente komme, og øynene hennes var like hvite av grå stær som skjorten min. Ingen ville hjelpe henne. Hun presset seg gjennom mengden og ropte, "Pappa! Pappa!" slik.
Jeg tenkte, stakkars gamle sjel. Jeg bestemte meg for å se hva hun ville. Ingen kjente meg igjen. Jeg fulgte med på vaktene som prøvde å komme seg gjennom mengden. Så jeg kom meg nærmere der hun var. Hver gang hun beveget seg en vei, beveget jeg meg samme vei, for å stå rett foran henne. Det virket ufint å gjøre det slik.
Til slutt støtet hun rett inn i meg. Hun sa, "Unnskyld."
Jeg svarte, "Ja, frue."
Da hun hørte stemmen min, sa hun, "Unnskyld, herr."
Jeg spurte, "Hva er i veien?"
Hun svarte, "Herr, jeg har mistet pappa. Jeg finner ham ikke." Hun la til, "Jeg er blind, og jeg kan ikke finne pappa."
Jeg spurte, "Hvor er du fra?"
Hun svarte, "Jeg er fra Memphis."

text:
And I looked over and I seen the chartered bus sitting there from Memphis. I thought, I can get her back there before them ushers… If I can't, I will let one of them get her over there."
She said, "I come over here from Memphis." And said, "I lost my daddy."
I said, "Well, I don't see any more colored people in this crowd of people."
Said, "Oh, will you help me to get to the bus?"
And I said, "What did you come over here for?"
She said, "I come over to see the healer."
I said, "The what?"
She said, "The healer."
And I said, "Do you believe that?" I thought I'd test her faith right good, you know.
I said, "Do you believe that?"
She said, "Yes, sir, I believe it."
And I said… Well, that made me feel little, you know. So I said, "Well, do you believe in the days of all these great fine doctors and hospitals, that God would send an Angel down to earth and would heal people?"
She said, "Yes, sir, I believes it."
I said, "How did you ever hear about it?"
text_no:
Jeg så på den chartrede bussen fra Memphis som sto der. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg kan få henne tilbake dit før vaktene kommer... Hvis ikke, kan jeg la en av dem føre henne dit."
Hun sa: "Jeg kom hit fra Memphis." Og fortsatte: "Jeg mistet min pappa."
Jeg sa: "Vel, jeg ser ingen andre fargede mennesker i denne mengden."
Hun spurte: "Kan du hjelpe meg til bussen?"
Jeg spurte: "Hva kom du hit for?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg kom for å se helbrederen."
"Helbrederen?" svarte jeg.
"Ja, helbrederen," bekreftet hun.
Jeg spurte: "Tror du på det?" Jeg ville teste hennes tro ordentlig.
"Ja, sir, jeg tror på det," sa hun.
Det fikk meg til å føle meg litt liten. Så jeg spurte: "Tror du, med alle disse store legene og sykehusene, at Gud ville sende en Engel til jorden for å helbrede folk?"
"Ja, sir, det tror jeg," svarte hun.
Så spurte jeg: "Hvordan hørte du om det?"

text:
She said, "I's hearing this morning on the radio a man up here at Kennett, Missouri, that had been blind ten years and he got his sight." That was him testifying on the radio, see. And she said, "I's come over, we formed a big committee and we come over by the bus." And she said, "They tell me this is the healer's last night here." And said, "I can't even get near the building." And said, "I've lost my father. I's blind. I just don't know what to do."
And I thought, Poor girl. She wasn't… She was in her teens---seventeen, eighteen years old. And I said, "What's wrong?"
She said, "Well, I got cataracts." And she said, "The doctor told me when they were ripe, he would take them off." But said, "Now since they got ripe…" Said, "He says he can't take them off, because they're wrapped around the optical nerve of my eyes. If he'd take it out, he'd have to take the optical nerve with it."
And I said, "And you believe that that healer would heal you?"
She said, "Yes, sir. I believe it."
And I said, "Well look at the doctor."
She said, "They can't help me."
And I thought of that song of old blind Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others thou art calling,
Do not pass me by.
And I thought of it. I said, "Oh, you don't believe that kind of stuff, do you?" I questioned her again.
She said, "Sir," she said, "I perceive that you are a white man. I believe that you're a white man."
I said, "Yes ma'am."
text_no:
Hun sa: "Jeg hørte på radioen i morges om en mann fra Kennett, Missouri, som hadde vært blind i ti år og fikk synet tilbake." Det var han som vitnet på radioen, skjønner du. Hun sa videre: "Vi dannet en stor komité og kom hit med buss. De forteller meg at dette er helbrederens siste kveld her, og jeg kan ikke engang komme nær bygningen. Jeg har mistet min far, jeg er blind, og jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre."
Jeg tenkte, stakkars jente. Hun var i tenårene, sytten eller atten år gammel. Jeg spurte: "Hva er problemet?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg har grå stær. Legen sa at når de var modne, kunne han fjerne dem. Men nå som de er modne, sier han at han ikke kan ta dem bort fordi de er viklet rundt synsnerven. Hvis han fjerner dem, må han også fjerne synsnerven."
Jeg spurte: "Og du tror at helbrederen kan helbrede deg?"
Hun svarte: "Ja, herr. Det tror jeg."
Jeg sa: "Hva med legen da?"
Hun sa: "De kan ikke hjelpe meg."
Jeg tenkte på den gamle sangen av blinde Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others Thou art calling,
Do not pass me by.
Jeg sa: "Å, du tror ikke på slikt, gjør du?" Jeg stilte henne spørsmålet en gang til.
Hun svarte: "Herr, jeg oppfatter at du er en hvit mann. Jeg tror at du er en hvit mann."
Jeg sa: "Ja, frue."

text:
She said, "If you'll just help me in to where that man's at that's praying for the sick, I'll be able to find my daddy after that." Oh-h!
That was too much for me. I thought I'd been hypocrite long enough. I said, "Look, lady, now keep… Hold your peace." I said, "Do you believe that guy's the healer?"
She said, "I believe Jesus is the healer."
I said, "That's right." I said, "But I'm Brother Branham."
She said, "Is you the healer?"
And I said… She grabbed me right by the lapels, like that, of the coat.
She said, "Is you the healer?"
I said, "No, you said Jesus was the healer."
She said, "Yes sir, but---but---but---but---but you is the Parson Branham?"
I said, "Yes, ma'am."
And she said, "Oh, have mercy on me, Parson Branham." She said, "Lord, how did this ever happen?"
text_no:
Hun sa, "Hvis du bare hjelper meg til det stedet der mannen som ber for de syke er, vil jeg kunne finne min far etterpå." Åh! Det var for mye for meg. Jeg tenkte at jeg hadde vært hykler lenge nok. Jeg sa, "Hør her, frue, nå… Hold fred." Jeg sa, "Tror du at den mannen er helbrederen?"
Hun sa, "Jeg tror at Jesus er helbrederen."
Jeg sa, "Det stemmer." Jeg sa, "Men jeg er Bror Branham."
Hun sa, "Er du helbrederen?"
Så… Hun grep meg rett i jakkeslagene.
Hun sa, "Er du helbrederen?"
Jeg sa, "Nei, du sa at Jesus var helbrederen."
Hun sa, "Ja, sir, men... men... men... men... men er du Pastor Branham?"
Jeg sa, "Ja, frue."
Og hun sa, "Åh, ha barmhjertighet med meg, Pastor Branham." Hun sa, "Herre, hvordan kunne dette skje?"

text:
See? Out there, and how did this ever be? God knows how to do it. And I said, "If you'll turn loose of my coat now." I said… And I couldn't pull it. No, sir. She wasn't going to turn me loose. No, sir. She was holding me right tight.
She said, "No, sir." She said, "I might lose you here."
And I said, "No, I will pray for you," see.
And I said, "Let me have your hand just a minute. I want to hold your hand while I pray for you." I said, "You bow your head now." And she did. And I prayed something like this.
text_no:
Ser du? Der ute, og hvordan kunne dette skje? Gud vet hvordan Han skal gjøre det. Jeg sa: "Hvis du slipper taket i frakken min nå," sa jeg... Men jeg kunne ikke få henne til å slippe. Nei, sir. Hun hadde ikke tenkt å slippe meg. Nei, sir. Hun holdt meg fast.
Hun sa: "Nei, sir. Jeg kan miste deg her."
Jeg sa: "Nei, jeg skal be for deg." 
Jeg fortsatte: "La meg få holde hånden din et øyeblikk. Jeg vil holde hånden din mens jeg ber for deg. Bøy hodet ditt nå." Og det gjorde hun. Så ba jeg omtrent slik:

text:
I said, "O Dear God, some nineteen hundred years ago an old rugged cross come dragging down through Jerusalem, dragging out the bloody footprints of the bearer. On the road up to Calvary, the weight was so heavy, the big old cross on His little weak shoulders, it rubbed it till He fell under the load. Then along come Simon the Cyrene---colored man---picked it up and helped Him bear the cross." I said, "Lord Jesus here's one of his children tonight staggering here in total blackness and darkness. She can't see her hand before her. Lord, You know what it meant for that man to take that load off of Your shoulder that day, and to help You to bear that cross." I said, "God, You bore her sickness at the same time You was bearing that cross up yonder." I said, "I ask Thee now…"
And just about that time something come over me. I knowed it had happened. I knowed it was gone. I waited just a minute.
Now, wonder as Brother Baxter gives you the instructions in the afternoon here. When a demon… See, a growth is a demon. And it's living, building cells. And just the life leaves it, but it will shrink. Then after a while it will swell and get bigger than it ever was. But the life's gone out of it. It'll leave you, see.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Å, kjære Gud, for omtrent 1900 år siden slepte et gammelt, røft kors ned gjennom Jerusalem, etterlot blodige fotspor langs veien.
